title: Spencerian business hand
subtitle: The faster, more compressed Spencerian script used for practical correspondence
excerpt: Platt Rogers Spencer designed two related scripts: the slow, rounded script of his showpiece copybooks, and the faster, slightly compressed business hand intended for daily commercial use. The business hand uses the same oval-and-shade discipline but writes at higher speed, with fewer of the decorative extensions that make showpiece work beautiful but impractical for a full letter.
sourceNotes: Platt Rogers Spencer, Spencerian Key to Practical Penmanship (1866). The Spencer Brothers' copybooks and business penmanship manuals (from 1848). Both public domain. The business variant is discussed explicitly in Spencer's own notes as distinct from the decorative hand. Internet Archive scans available.

glossaryTerms.definition: The fundamental unit of Spencerian letterforms: a small, precise ellipse inclined at 52 degrees from the baseline that forms the basis of every rounded letter in the script.
The hairline upstroke that joins one letter to the next in a connected script, beginning from the baseline and rising to the entry point of the following letter.
A stroke that changes direction mid-arc, combining a left-curving lower half with a right-curving upper half. The transition between the two curves occurs at the baseline in Spencerian letters.

content.text: The business hand is Spencerian written at roughly twice the speed of the showpiece hand. The letterforms compress slightly to about seven-eighths of their showpiece width; the long, oval-based entry strokes shorten; the finish strokes lose their extended terminals. The
spencerian oval
remains at 52 degrees and the shade rule is unchanged: pressure only on the downstroke.
What you need

Nib: Nikko G, a moderately flexible pointed nib suited to a higher writing speed than the very flexible Hunt 101. The Nikko G opens cleanly without requiring as much pressure as the Hunt, which suits the faster stroke pace of the business hand.
Ink: sumi or iron-gall at writing consistency. Rule guide lines at x-height and ascender height, with a light slant guide at 52 degrees.
Nib angle and pen hold

Hold the pen at 54 degrees from the baseline. Oblique holder with a flange that aligns the nib with the Spencerian slant axis. The hand rests on the side of the little finger; movement is from the wrist and lower arm.
Key differences from the showpiece hand
Entry strokes: in the showpiece hand, the
connective stroke
is long and broad. In the business hand it is short, beginning from mid-x-height rather than from below the baseline.
Compound curves: the
compound curve
in letters n and m is tighter, with the lower curve shallower and the upper curve less pronounced.
Finish strokes: letters that end with a curved exit (a, d, e, g) shorten the exit to just reach the baseline rather than continuing in a full oval loop.
Capitals: capitals are written unadorned, without the swept entry strokes and flourishes of the showpiece hand.
Common mistakes

attrs.symptom: Writing looks rushed and irregular rather than compact and consistent
attrs.cause: Speed has been increased but the oval discipline has been abandoned.

content.text: Slow down to the showpiece pace, verify the ovals are consistent, then gradually increase speed. The business hand is a compression of the showpiece, not a separate script written faster.

attrs.cause: Pressure is applied at the start of the downstroke rather than building through the stroke.

content.text: Fade the shade in at the entry point and fade it out before the exit. A sudden nib-spread at the beginning of a fast downstroke is the most common cause of spattering.
